text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Hello and welcome back toi’s live coverage of the2024 Olympic Games in Paris.

USA’s Noah Lyles claimed a stunning win in the men’s 100m final afterTeam GB’sZharnel Hughes and Louie Hinchliffe saw their bid for gold come to an end in the semis.

The women’s 200m also got under way today, with Daryll Neita and Dina Asher-Smith both qualifying for Monday’s semi-finals. Their teammate Josh Kerr is through to the men’s 1500m final too.

Elsewhere,Novak DjokovicbeatCarlos Alcarazto win an elusive Olympic gold medal, while GB’s Tommy Fleetwood won silver as Scottie Scheffler was crowned champion at Le Golf National.

This live blog has now closed.

All hail the new king in town.

Take a bow, Noah Lyles.

The American talked the talk and he walked the walk in the men’s 100m final.

You can read James Gray’s report from Stade de France here.

We’ll be back tomorrow to see if Team GB can add more medals to their haul.

Noah Lyles was the slowest of anyone out of the blocks and still last after 30 metres, but finished fastest to pip Kishane Thompson in a seven-way photo finish.

“It’s the one I wanted. It’s the hard battle. It’s the amazing opponents. Everybody’s healthy, everybody came prepared for the fight and I want to prove that I’m the man amongst all of them. I’m the wolf among wolves,” said Lyles, whose USA team-mate Fred Kerley took bronze.

“I went up to Kishane and I was like ‘Imma be honest bro, I think you had that one’ and I was fully expecting to see his name pop up.

“To see my name pop, I was like ‘Goodness gracious, I’m incredible’.”

Lyles is also the reigning world champion in the 200m, and will start as a considerable favourite to do the double.

“That’s my better event,” Lyes added.

“And now that I’ve got a new PR [personal record] in the 100m, I’m ready to take it to the 200m.”

Noah Lyles gives his thumbs up to VAR, Olympic 100m champion by a nipple.

Seven-way photo finish… and Noah Lyles has won it!

USA’s Lyles has beaten Kishane Thompson by five thousandths of a second in a time of 9.784, with Fred Kerley taking bronze for the Americans.

The runners have been standing there for about two minutes waiting for the song to finish…

This is a bit of a joke.

Right, the ponytailed, mustachioed Ethan Katzberg (it’s not a mullet, the honourable New Zealander to my right tells me, and he would know) has won hammer gold for Canada, which means we only have one event left.

The men’s 100m. The blue riband event of the Olympic Games.

There are three Americans in the final, with world champion Noah Lyles the fastest of the three this year, although 29-year-old Fred Kerley has been as fast as 9.76.

The favourite though is Kishane Thompson, 23, aiming to bring gold back to Jamaica for the first time since Usain Bolt in Rio 2016, and his team-mate Oblique Seville, also 23 years old, could well grab a medal too.

Defending champion Marcell Jacobs is out in lane nine, one of the slowest qualifiers, and the youngest man out there is Letsile Tebogo of Botswana.

The starter’s pistol is due to go any second…

That will be two Brits in the 1500m final then: Neil Gourley wins the second semi-final in 3:32.11.

Azeddine Habz, the Frenchman, finished last, much to the chagrin of the home crowd, who have not had a great time of it on the track so far: still medal-less in the athletics.

The Italians next to me are standing up clapping because Pietro Arese has snuck into the final in sixth.

At the front, Jakob Ingebrigtsen and Josh Kerr cross practically together to qualify for Tuesday’s final with ease.

George Mills, son of England full-back Danny, is run out of it in 11th: he had to run in the repechage so you can forgive a little tiredness.

There is a high jump final going on, although someone should tell world record holder Yaroslava Mahuchikh.

She is in a battle for gold with Nicola Olyslagers of Australia, but is currently asleep on the infield in a sleeping bag. What the actual.

Hodgkinson looks like she has GEARS.

Wins her semi-final at a canter, easing down at the end but still registering the fastest time of the round.

She will run for gold at 8.47pm tomorrow night.

As cool as you like from Hodgkinson. She controlled the race from the start before turning on the afterburners down the home straight. Same again tomorrow night, please…

Not a great start to the 800m semi-finals for Great Britain: Phoebe Gill finished fourth in the first one and is already out of the fastest loser spots, and Jemma Reekie never got into them, finishing fifth. Almost everyone in front of her ran a PB, to be fair.

Keely Hodgkinson to come in the third semi-final, the odds-on favourite for the gold medal in lifetime best form this year.

Her biggest rival for the medal, Mary Moraa of Kenya, is already through.

Adam Peaty enters his latest hiatus with a scorching warning to anti-doping agencies to root out the cheats.

The three-time Olympic champion was part of the men’s 4x100m medley relay team which finished fourth in a race won by China.

In the Chinese team were Qin Haiyang and Sun Jiajun, both of whom had tested positive for trimetazidine ahead of the Tokyo Olympics, only to escape sanction from Chinada (the Chinese anti-doping agency).

The agency concluded that the readings were caused by “an isolated mass incident caused by athletes’ unknowing consumption of food contaminated with TMZ,” and “no fault or negligence”.

World Anti-Doping Agency (Wada) had previously confirmed toThe New York Timesthat three Chinese swimmers had previously tested positive for ‘trace amounts’ of banned drug clenbuterol – with a later anti-doping report naming Qin as one of the three swimmers.

And Peaty, who finished fourth alongside Oliver Morgan, Duncan Scott, and Matt Richards, believes swimmers who have been found to have been contaminated on more than one occasion should be banned from the sport for good.

He said: “One of my favourite quotes I’ve seen lately is ‘There’s no point in winning, if you’re not winning fair’. I think you know that truth in your heart. If you touch and you know you’re cheating, you’re not winning.

“For me, if you have been on that and have been contaminated twice, as an honourable person, you should be out of the sport. But we know sport isn’t that simple. I’ve also been asked about people who haven’t been contaminated and I respect that.

“I don’t want to paint a whole nation or a whole group of people with one brush, that would be very unfair.”

Jamaica’s Kishane Thompson wins the third semi-final in 9.80 and reinforces his status as gold medal favourite.

Coincidentally, that is the same time as Marcell Jacobs ran to win gold in 2021 – and the Italian will have a chance to do it again after qualifying by 0.03 seconds.

Zharnel Hughes finishes 14th overall having run 10.01 seconds. He has still not been under 10 seconds in what has been an injury-troubled season.

No luck for Louie Hinchliffe in the second semi-final. Akani Simbine of South Africa and Botswana’s Letsile Simbine are through automatically, while defending champion Marcell Jacobs and Kenny Bednarek are now “in the hot seat” of the losers’ places. They ran 9.92 and 9.93 respectively.

Zharnel Hughes, up next, is Britain’s last remaining hope of ending a 32-year streak without a medal in the men’s 100m.

Meanwhile at the swimming, Team GB have finished fourth in the 4x100m medley relay – and Adam Peaty has said afterwards that he will be taking another break from the sport.

“Tomorrow is never promised and if my heart wants it my heart wants it and I will sign that contract with myself to do it,” he said.

“That could be a long way away, but I think I’ve got to step away from the sport because it hurt too much this time.

“I’m going to take some time off everything and I don’t really know what the answer is. I’m taking time, if my family needs me they need me. I don’t know you can’t lie to your heart but if it wants it, it wants it.”

Do not adjust your TV sets, that really is what Louie Hinchliffe’s hair looks like.

The man with the mullet is the hottest British sprinting sensation, was a junior golfer until he was 17 and has now switched to athletics and is coached by the great Carl Lewis.

He is Great Britain’s first of two semi-finalists, sandwiched between USA’s Noah Lyles and Jamaica’s Oblique Seville.

Third for Hinchliffe in 9.97 seconds, only 0.02 secs outside his personal best.

Lyles is beaten again: it was Hinchliffe in the heats and it was Seville of Jamaica this time, who ran a personal best of 9.81.

There were no Jamaicans in the final three years ago, but there will be at least one this time.

Hinchliffe, by the way, is now waiting on a fastest loser’s spot, of which there are two. He will be praying for a slow semi-final to follow…

Adam Peaty was unable to inspire Team GB to a podium place as they finished fourth in the men’s 4×100m medley relay final.

While Peaty, who on Saturday described how he had had the “worst week” of his life physically after testing positive for Covid on Monday, left Team GB second at halfway, they fell away thereafter.

China took top spot ahead of the United States with France third – more than a second ahead of Team GB.

A moment that will be spoken about for days to come.This is the controversial moment GB’s Amber Rutter hit the clay on the right-hand-side… but it wasn’t counted!#Olympics#BBCOlympics#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/ePOrliAapl

Here comes the British No 2 Charlie Dobson, who has run his personal best of 44.23 this year.

He probably won’t need to run quite that quick to qualify, but he will need to overcome the noise produced by a crowd roaring on France’s Gilles Biron on his inside.

Dobson is through, winning the heat in 44.96. A very relaxed win for the “Ginger Ninja” (it really says that on his bio, I’m not making it up).

He’s also got an aeronautical engineering degree, so I’m disappointed his nickname isn’t “Rocketman” or something.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: Olympics 2024: Live updates from Sunday in Paris as Team GB chase more gold
author: Evan Bartlett, Tom Ward
description: Follow i's live blog for updates from the Olympic Games in Paris
